name: log
context: Inspect commit history with filtering, formatting, graph visualization, and search across branches
info: Git log is the primary tool for navigating commit history. It shows commits in reverse chronological order with powerful filtering (by author, date, path, message), formatting (oneline, graph, custom pretty-print), and search (grep messages, diff content with -S and -G). Agents use log constantly to understand what changed, who changed it, when, and why. Key use cases: reviewing recent changes before pushing, understanding the history of a specific file, finding which commit introduced a bug (often used with git bisect), and generating changelogs. The --graph flag visualizes branch topology. See [[git/diff]] for viewing actual code changes, [[git/commit]] for creating the history you inspect here, and [[git/branch]] for understanding branch divergence in log output.
triggers: find which commit introduced a bug
body:
# Git Log

Git log is the window into your project's history. Every commit ever made is accessible through log.

## Basic Usage

```bash
# Show full log (reverse chronological)
git log

# Compact one-line format
git log --oneline

# Last N commits
git log -5
git log --oneline -10

# Show with diff
git log -p
git log -p -1  # last commit with full diff
```

## Graph Visualization

```bash
# ASCII branch graph
git log --oneline --graph

# Graph with all branches
git log --oneline --graph --all

# Graph with decoration (branch/tag labels)
git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate
```

## Filtering

```bash
# By author
git log --author="Alice"
git log --author="alice@example.com"

# By date
git log --since="2026-01-01"
git log --after="2 weeks ago"
git log --until="2026-03-01"
git log --since="2026-01-01" --until="2026-03-01"

# By file/path
git log -- src/auth.js
git log --oneline -- src/

# By commit message (grep)
git log --grep="fix auth"
git log --grep="BREAKING" --all-match

# By content change (pickaxe — find when string was added/removed)
git log -S "functionName"
git log -S "TODO" --oneline

# By regex content change
git log -G "def\s+process_.*"

# Commits on one branch but not another
git log main..feature
git log feature --not main  # equivalent

# Merge commits only
git log --merges

# Exclude merge commits
git log --no-merges
```

## Custom Formatting

```bash
# Pretty format
git log --pretty=format:"%h %an %ar %s"

# Common format placeholders
# %H  — full hash
# %h  — short hash
# %an — author name
# %ae — author email
# %ar — author date (relative)
# %ad — author date
# %s  — subject
# %b  — body
# %d  — decorations (branches, tags)

# Example: changelog format
git log --pretty=format:"- %s (%h)" v1.0..v2.0

# Shortlog (grouped by author)
git shortlog -sn
git shortlog -sn --since="2026-01-01"
```

## Git Blame

Show who last modified each line of a file:

```bash
# Full blame
git blame src/auth.js

# Blame specific lines
git blame -L 10,20 src/auth.js

# Ignore whitespace changes
git blame -w src/auth.js

# Show original commit (before moves/copies)
git blame -C src/auth.js
```

## Git Bisect

Binary search through history to find which commit introduced a bug:

```bash
# Start bisect
git bisect start

# Mark current as bad
git bisect bad

# Mark a known good commit
git bisect good v1.0

# Git checks out a middle commit — test it, then:
git bisect good  # or
git bisect bad

# Repeat until git finds the culprit
# When done:
git bisect reset

# Automated bisect with a test script
git bisect start HEAD v1.0
git bisect run npm test
```

## Comparing Branches

```bash
# Commits in feature not in main
git log main..feature --oneline

# Commits in either but not both
git log main...feature --oneline

# With left-right markers
git log --left-right main...feature --oneline
```

## Agent Best Practices

1. **Use --oneline for scanning** — faster to parse programmatically
2. **Use --graph --all for orientation** — shows full branch topology
3. **Filter by path** — `git log -- path/to/file` for file-specific history
4. **Use -S for archaeology** — find when a function or variable was added/removed
5. **Use blame for line-level attribution** — know who changed what and when
6. **Use bisect for bug hunting** — binary search is faster than manual inspection

sections.title: Basic log commands
sections.body: `git log` shows full history. `git log --oneline` shows compact format. `git log -N` limits to N commits. `git log -p` includes diffs. `git log --stat` shows files changed per commit. These are the starting points for any history inspection.

sections.title: Filtering commits
sections.body: Filter by author (`--author`), date range (`--since`/`--until`), path (`-- path`), message (`--grep`), or content change (`-S` string, `-G` regex). Combine filters to narrow results. Use `--all-match` to AND multiple `--grep` patterns. Use `main..feature` to see commits in feature not in main (branch comparison).

sections.title: Graph visualization
sections.body: `git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate` is the most informative view of branch topology. The graph shows merge points, branch divergence, and where each branch/tag points. This is essential for understanding the current state of a multi-branch repository.

sections.title: Git blame for line-level attribution
sections.body: `git blame <file>` annotates each line with the commit, author, and date of last modification. Use `-L start,end` for specific line ranges. Use `-w` to ignore whitespace. Use `-C` to detect code moved between files. Blame is essential for understanding why code exists in its current form and who to ask about it.

sections.title: Binary search with git bisect
sections.body: `git bisect start`, mark `good` and `bad` commits, then git binary-searches through history. At each step, test the checkout and mark good or bad. Automated: `git bisect run <test-command>` runs the test script at each step. Bisect finds the exact commit that introduced a regression in O(log n) steps. Always `git bisect reset` when done.

sections.title: Custom output formats
sections.body: `git log --pretty=format:"..."` with placeholders: %H (hash), %h (short hash), %an (author), %ae (email), %ar (relative date), %s (subject), %b (body), %d (decorations). `git shortlog -sn` groups by author with counts. Custom formats are useful for generating changelogs, reports, and machine-parseable output.

links.context: View actual code changes referenced in log
Log shows branch topology and divergence

related.note: Log shows commit metadata; diff shows content changes
Log reads the history that commits create
Bisect and reflog are key troubleshooting tools

scripts.name: History Search
scripts.description: Search commit history with various filters
scripts.inline:
#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -euo pipefail

MODE="${1:?Usage: history-search.sh <recent|file|search|author|graph> [args...]}"
shift

case "$MODE" in
  recent)
    N="${1:-10}"
    git log --oneline -"$N"
    ;;
  file)
    FILE="${1:?File path required}"
    echo "History for $FILE:"
    git log --oneline --follow -- "$FILE"
    ;;
  search)
    TERM="${1:?Search term required}"
    echo "Commits matching: $TERM"
    git log --oneline --grep="$TERM"
    ;;
  author)
    AUTHOR="${1:?Author name/email required}"
    echo "Commits by $AUTHOR:"
    git log --oneline --author="$AUTHOR"
    ;;
  graph)
    git log --oneline --graph --all --decorate -20
    ;;
  *)
    echo "Unknown mode: $MODE"
    echo "Usage: history-search.sh <recent|file|search|author|graph> [args...]"
    exit 1
    ;;
esac

path.description: File or directory path for file-scoped history

query.description: Search term for grep or pickaxe

author: dojo-community
